title: Cluster Troubleshooting
description: Diagnose and fix various cluster issues including pod failures, node problems, and networking issues
context: The cluster has several issues that need to be identified and resolved. Use your troubleshooting skills to restore normal operation.

tasks.title: Fix failing pod
tasks.description: There's a pod named 'broken-app' in the 'production' namespace that's not starting. Identify and fix the issue.

commands: kubectl get pod broken-app -n production --no-headers | grep Running

hints: Use 'kubectl describe pod broken-app -n production' to see events
Check the pod's YAML configuration for errors
Common issues: wrong image name, missing secrets, resource constraints

tasks.title: Resolve node NotReady status
tasks.description: One of the worker nodes shows as 'NotReady'. Investigate and fix the issue.

commands: kubectl get nodes --no-headers | grep NotReady | wc -l

hints: Use 'kubectl describe node <node-name>' to see the issue
Check kubelet status with 'systemctl status kubelet'
Look at kubelet logs with 'journalctl -u kubelet'

tasks.title: Fix DNS resolution
tasks.description: Pods cannot resolve DNS names. Fix the CoreDNS issue.

commands: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns --no-headers | grep Running | wc -l

hints: Check CoreDNS pods in kube-system namespace
Look at CoreDNS logs for errors

tasks.title: Check etcd health
tasks.description: Verify that etcd cluster is healthy and accessible

commands: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=etcd --no-headers | grep Running

hints: Check etcd pod status in kube-system namespace
Use etcdctl to check cluster health

resources.name: broken-pod
resources.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: broken-app
  namespace: production
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:nonexistent-tag
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "10Gi"
        cpu: "8"

resources.name: debug-commands
resources.content:
# Useful debugging commands:
# kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n <namespace>

# kubectl get events --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
# systemctl status kubelet
# journalctl -u kubelet
# kubectl get nodes -o wide
